commit.message:
feat(patterns): fractal-worktree + backprop-merge patterns (TinyAGI/fractals port)

Source: https://github.com/TinyAGI/fractals (MIT)
Decision: port 2 key innovations to SF's Python/AgentExecutor stack instead
of running external Claude/Codex CLI.

Pattern #16 — fractal-worktree (platform/patterns/impls/fractal_worktree.py):
- Phase 1 PLAN: LLM classify gate (ATOMIC vs COMPOSITE) before decomposing
- Recursive decompose() builds a leaf tree (max_depth=3, max_children=5)
- Phase 2 EXECUTE: leaf tasks run in parallel, each in an isolated git worktree
  (git worktree add) to prevent parallel agent file conflicts
- Graceful degradation: if no git repo → plain parallel execution

Pattern #17 — backprop-merge (platform/patterns/impls/backprop_merge.py):
- Same PLAN phase as fractal-worktree
- After leaf execution: merge agent synthesizes results bottom-up
  leaf1+leaf2 → merge(composite1) → ... → merge(root)
- Resolves conflicts between parallel agents; produces coherent final output
- Source: TinyAGI/fractals roadmap "Backpropagation (merge agent)"

Wiring:
- engine.py: dispatch cases for "fractal-worktree" and "backprop-merge" types
- store.py: PatternDef seeds for both patterns
- registry.yaml: fractal-worktree module card with source + rationale comment

Tests: 18 offline tests in tests/test_fractal_patterns.py
(parse_subtasks, classify prompt, FractalNode tree, git detection, merge prompt)

Co-authored-by: Copilot <223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>

commit.message:
fix: detect EOF at exact _RANGE_WINDOW boundary via Content-Range header

When a file's total size is an exact multiple of _RANGE_WINDOW (4 MB),
the window loop received exactly _RANGE_WINDOW bytes in a 206 response,
so the `window_got < _RANGE_WINDOW` guard did not fire. The loop then
requested the next window and hit a 416 Range Not Satisfiable, raised
as MediaNotFoundError even though the stream was fully delivered.

Fix: add `_is_content_range_eof(headers, window_end)` static helper that
parses `Content-Range: bytes start-end/total` and returns True when
window_end >= total - 1. Called after the size check in get_audio_stream;
malformed or absent headers fall through safely to the next window.

Add `test_get_audio_stream_exact_window_boundary` that patches
_RANGE_WINDOW to 16 bytes, produces a 16-byte file with a matching
Content-Range header, and asserts only one HTTP request is made.
Also add `headers` parameter to `_MockResponse` for this and future tests.

Closes: https://github.com/music-assistant/server/pull/3237#discussion_r2889278609

Co-authored-by: Copilot <223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>

commit.message:
docs: Expand Introduction page with value proposition and architecture (#1609)

The Introduction page was a single sentence with no explanation of *why*
Manael exists or how to deploy it effectively. Expanded both the English
and Japanese pages to be genuinely useful to evaluators and new users.

### Changes

- **`website/content/en/docs/introduction.md`**
  - Added context on the cost of pre-generating image variants at scale
- **Key Concepts** section: on-the-fly optimization,
`Accept`-header-based content negotiation, stateless design
- **Architecture** section: CDN → Manael → Origin Storage placement with
cache-miss flow explanation

- **`website/content/ja/docs/introduction.md`**
- Full Japanese equivalent of the above, with localized section titles
(`主な特徴`, `アーキテクチャ`)

<!-- START COPILOT ORIGINAL PROMPT -->



<details>

<summary>Original prompt</summary>

> 
> ----
> 
> *This section details on the original issue you should resolve*
> 
> <issue_title>Docs: Expand "Introduction" page to better explain value
proposition and architecture</issue_title>
> <issue_description>### Background
> The current "Introduction" page is very brief. While it states what
Manael does (converts JPEG/PNG to WebP), it doesn't adequately explain
*why* users need it, what problems it solves, or its core architectural
strengths.
> We should expand this page to provide a more comprehensive overview
without relying on comparisons to commercial SaaS alternatives.
> 
> ### Proposal
> Rewrite the `introduction.md` files (English and Japanese) to cover
the following key points:
> 1. The challenge of modern image optimization (storage costs, format
fragmentation).
> 2. How Manael solves this via on-the-fly processing and content
negotiation.
> 3. Its stateless architecture and optimal placement (Origin -> Manael
-> CDN).
> 
> ### Tasks
> - Update `website/content/en/docs/introduction.md`
> - Update `website/content/ja/docs/introduction.md`
> 
> ---
> 
> ### Suggested Content (English)
> 
> ```markdown
> ---
> title: Introduction
> weight: 1
> ---
> 
> Manael is an open-source, high-performance HTTP proxy for on-the-fly
image processing.
> 
> In modern web development, serving highly optimized images is crucial
for improving page load speeds, reducing bandwidth costs, and boosting
SEO scores. However, pre-generating and storing every permutation of an
image (multiple sizes, WebP, AVIF) can quickly bloat your storage and
complicate your asset management pipeline.
> 
> Manael solves this problem by processing images in real-time.
> 
> ### Key Concepts
> 
> * **On-the-fly Optimization:** Manael dynamically converts original
images (such as large JPEGs or PNGs) into next-generation formats like
WebP and AVIF the moment they are requested.
> * **Smart Content Negotiation:** By analyzing the `Accept` header of
the incoming HTTP request, Manael automatically determines and serves
the best image format supported by the user's browser, while seamlessly
falling back to the original format for older clients.
> * **Stateless by Design:** Manael does not store or cache any images
itself. It acts purely as a processing layer. This makes it incredibly
lightweight and easy to scale horizontally in containerized environments
like Kubernetes or Cloud Run.
> 
> ### Architecture
> 
> Manael is designed to sit transparently between your origin storage
and your caching layer.
> 
> The optimal architecture is to place Manael behind a Content Delivery
Network (CDN) and in front of your storage bucket (e.g., Amazon S3,
Google Cloud Storage) or origin server.
> 
> `[Client] --> [CDN] --> [Manael Proxy] --> [Origin Storage]`
> 
> When a user requests an image, the CDN checks its cache. If it's a
cache miss, the request falls back to Manael, which fetches the original
image from the origin, processes it, and returns it. The CDN then caches
this optimized response for all future requests.
> ```
> 
> ---
> 
> ### Suggested Content (Japanese)
> 
> ```markdown
> ---
> title: Manaelとは
> weight: 1
> ---
> 
> Manael は、オンザフライで画像処理を行うオープンソースの高性能な HTTP プロキシサーバーです。
> 
>
最新のWeb開発において、最適化された画像を配信することは、ページ表示速度の向上や帯域幅コストの削減、SEOスコアの改善において不可欠です。しかし、デバイスに合わせた複数のサイズや、WebP・AVIFといった次世代フォーマットの画像をすべて事前に生成し保存しておくことは、ストレージ容量の無駄遣いとなり、アセット管理を複雑にします。
> 
> Manael は、リクエスト時にリアルタイムで画像を処理することでこの問題を解決します。
> 
> ### 主な特徴
> 
> * **オンザフライ最適化:** リクエストが来た瞬間に、オリジナルの画像（JPEGやPNGなど）を WebP や AVIF
などの軽量な次世代フォーマットへ動的に変換します。
> * **スマートなコンテントネゴシエーション:** クライアントからのHTTPリクエストに含まれる `Accept`
ヘッダーを解析し、ブラウザが対応している最適なフォーマットを自動的に判断して配信します。古いブラウザには元のフォーマットをそのまま返すため、互換性の心配もありません。
> * **ステートレスな設計:** Manael
自身は画像を一切保存・キャッシュしません。純粋な処理レイヤーとして機能するため極めて軽量であり、Kubernetes や Cloud Run
などのコンテナ環境で簡単に水平スケールさせることができます。
> 
> ### アーキテクチャ
> 
> Manael は、オリジンストレージとキャッシュサーバーの間に透過的に配置されるよう設計されています。
> 
> 最も推奨される構成は、Amazon S3 などのオリジンストレージの手前に Manael を配置し、さらにその手前に
CDN（コンテンツ配信ネットワーク）を置くアーキテクチャです。
> 
> `[クライアント] --> [CDN] --> [Manael プロキシ] --> [オリジンストレージ]`
> 
> ユーザーが画像をリクエストした際、CDN にキャッシュがなければ Manael にリクエストが渡ります。Manael
はオリジンから元画像を取得して最適化処理を行い、レスポンスを返します。CDN はその最適化された画像をキャッシュするため、以降のリクエストは
CDN から高速に配信されます。
> ```</issue_description>
>

>

commit.message:
Implement foundational TLS (Thread-Local Storage) support

Implements partial TLS support as outlined in ROADMAP Phase 7.3.

New Features:
- PT_TLS segment detection and parsing in ELF parser
- TLS module registration and lifecycle management
- TLS block allocation with initialization (data + BSS)
- Support for TLS relocations:
  * R_X86_64_DTPMOD64 (module ID for General Dynamic model)
  * R_X86_64_DTPOFF64/32 (module-relative offsets)
  * R_X86_64_TPOFF64/32 (thread pointer offsets)

New Package:
- internal/tls: Complete TLS infrastructure
  * Module: TLS template management
  * Block: Per-thread TLS allocation
  * Manager: Global TLS module registry
  * TLS block allocation with proper memory mapping
  * Template data initialization

Implementation Details:
- elf/parse.go: Added PT_TLS segment detection
- loader/loader.go: TLS module registration during Load()
- loader/loader.go: TLS relocation processing
- dl/dl.go: __tls_get_addr placeholder for symbol resolution

Test Coverage:
- internal/tls/tls_test.go: Unit tests for TLS infrastructure
- dl/dl_test.go: Integration test for TLS library loading
- testdata/libtls.c: Test library with TLS variables

Limitations:
- __tls_get_addr runtime function not fully implemented
  (requires C-callable trampoline and runtime integration)
- Code sequence relocations (TLSGD, TLSLD, GOTTPOFF) not supported
- Single-threaded TLS block management (no per-thread DTV)

Status: Libraries with PT_TLS can be loaded and parsed successfully.
TLS data relocations work. Dynamic TLS access via __tls_get_addr
requires additional infrastructure.

Tests: All existing tests pass. New TLS tests added.
Complexity: applyRelaTable increased from 18.4 to 44.6 due to
comprehensive TLS relocation support (expected and acceptable).

commit.message:
kernel: fix IPC_NS and add missing container namespace deps

sunxi_defconfig disables CONFIG_SYSVIPC and CONFIG_POSIX_MQUEUE, which
are hard Kconfig dependencies of CONFIG_IPC_NS. Without them the IPC_NS
line in the overlay was silently dropped, so the built kernel had no IPC
namespace support. runc combines CLONE_NEWIPC with other namespace flags
in a single unshare(2) call; the missing IPC_NS caused EINVAL and the
container failed to start with:

  nsexec-1: failed to unshare remaining namespaces (except cgroupns):   Invalid argument

Fix by explicitly enabling the dependencies (SYSVIPC, POSIX_MQUEUE) so
IPC_NS actually takes effect. Also add the remaining options needed for
full runc/Docker compatibility:

- CONFIG_SYSVIPC=y        -- IPC_NS dependency
- CONFIG_POSIX_MQUEUE=y   -- IPC_NS dependency
- CONFIG_CGROUP_NS=y      -- cgroup namespaces (CLONE_NEWCGROUP)
- CONFIG_CPUSETS=y        -- Docker cpuset resource management

Co-authored-by: Copilot <223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>

commit.message:
fix(consensus): allow single-validator BFT and fix peer sync for all node types (#1767)

* feat(cbe): gate token deployment behind registered DID and 100 SOV minimum

- BondingCurveToken gains creator_did: String field (serde default for old data)
- apply_bonding_curve_deploy() rejects deployers with no on-chain identity
- apply_bonding_curve_deploy() rejects deployers with < 100 SOV balance
- API handler handle_deploy() enforces both gates before state mutation
- StateMutator gains get_token_balance() read-only helper
- creator_did set to did:zhtp:<hex(did_hash)> from verified identity

* fix(lib-network): add AsyncReadExt/AsyncWriteExt imports for graceful_shutdown

* fix(catchup-sync): skip fee validation when replaying peer-committed blocks

Catch-up sync downloads blocks that were already committed by a quorum.
Those blocks may contain transactions valid under older fee rules (fee=0).
The BlockExecutor's fee check rejected them, permanently stalling syncing
nodes at height 0.

Add BlockExecutor::new_trusted_replay() (skip_fee_validation=true),
Blockchain::apply_block_trusted_for_sync() that uses it temporarily,
and wire catchup_sync_from_peer to call the trusted path.

* Update lib-blockchain/src/blockchain.rs

Co-authored-by: Copilot <175728472+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>

* fix(consensus): allow single-validator BFT and fix peer sync for all node types

- Lower MIN_BFT_VALIDATORS from 4 to 1 in lib-types and lib-consensus so
  a single bootstrap validator can commit blocks via BFT without waiting
  for a full validator set
- Add BlockExecutor::from_config_trusted_replay() to skip fee validation
  when replaying already-committed peer blocks during catch-up sync
- Use trusted replay executor in all three sync/mod.rs import paths so
  fee=0 system transactions in genesis blocks do not abort initial sync
- Spawn peer sync loop for all node types (not just observers) so
  validator nodes can gap-fill after startup sync closes
- Fix stale test asserting MIN_BFT_VALIDATORS == 4

* fix(consensus): lower MIN_BFT_VALIDATORS to 1 in blockchain component

Aligns with lib-types and lib-consensus where MIN_BFT_VALIDATORS is
already 1. BFT consensus is now active from the first validator onward
instead of waiting for 4.

* Fix validator bootstrap startup and peer sync gating

* fix(consensus): enforce strict key validation and deterministic bootstrap proposer

* consensus: broadcast only to authenticated validator identities

* rebase: resolve conflict follow-up compile fixes

* fix: SOV balance shows 0 after registration and chain stall on empty mempool

- Executor: exempt system transactions (empty inputs) from fee validation,
  matching TransactionValidator logic; fixes fee=0 rejections for
  IdentityRegistration, WalletRegistration, ValidatorRegistration etc.
- Balance handlers: read SOV from in-memory token_contracts instead of
  SledStore; SledStore is not updated during registration (no active block
  transaction), causing 0 SOV to be returned despite successful mint
- Mining: allow empty blocks so chain never stalls when mempool is drained
- Blockchain: remove dead SledStore persist attempt in register_wallet()
  that always fails outside block processing

* fix(sync): replace export-based bootstrap sync with paginated block-range sync

- Drop single-shot /blockchain/export from startup path; it fails for
  chains >~700 blocks due to the 16 MB QUIC message-size limit
- Implement paginated initial sync using /api/v1/blockchain/blocks/{s}/{e}
  (200 blocks per page, same endpoint as consensus catch-up)
- Hard gate: if any peer reports height > 0 but all sync attempts fail,
  block mining/genesis creation and retry every 30 s — never fall through
  to forking from genesis
- Post-sync catch-up loop: after first sync pass, immediately re-check peer
  tip and drain any blocks mined during import (up to 10 rounds) so the
  node starts mining as close to the peer's current tip as possible
- Fix empty-store start: when SledStore is uninitialised, begin fetching
  from block 0 (genesis) not block 1
- Fix SOV balance queries: sync in-memory token_contracts from SledStore
  after each executor block commit (TokenTransfer/TokenMint touched addrs)
  so balance queries read a single authoritative in-memory source
- Remove stale max(mem, sled) balance hack from wallet and token handlers

* fix(consensus): enforce single bootstrap miner and leader startup gating

* fix(mining): allow any bootstrap validator DID to mine

* fix(consensus): prevent non-genesis nodes from mining startup blocks

All FullValidator nodes had can_mine=true, causing G2/G3/G4 to mine
their own wallet-registration and SOV-backfill blocks at startup
(bypassing is_bootstrap_mining_authority). This created unique block
hashes that diverged from G1's chain immediately after initial sync.

Fixes:
- Gate startup mining in run_post_bootstrap_sov_backfill on
  chain_height == 0: only the genesis node (G1) mines startup blocks;
  synced nodes defer transactions to the leader's mining loop.
- Add genesis gate in mod.rs: non-bootstrap-leader nodes loop and
  retry peer sync instead of falling through to genesis creation when
  all peers report height 0 at simultaneous startup.
- Add global catch-up trigger (blockchain_provider.rs) registered from
  consensus setup; fire it from network_blockchain_event_receiver when
  a non-consecutive block arrives so catch-up runs without waiting for
  BFT divergence detection.

* consensus: suppress partition evidence in single-validator bootstrap

* fix(identity): skip SOV backfill on non-genesis nodes to prevent mempool flood

Non-genesis nodes (G2/G3/G4) were creating TokenMint transactions for SOV
backfill and broadcasting them via mesh. Since each node signs with its own
key, the transactions had unique hashes and were not deduplicated, causing
4x mempool inflation (4549 pending txs) and stalling the G1 mining loop.

Fix: return early from run_post_bootstrap_sov_backfill if chain height > 0.
Synced nodes already have SOV balances from G1's mined blocks.

Also adds oracle read subcommands to CLI (status, price, config,
pending-updates, slashing-events, banned-validators).

* fix(identity): gate SOV backfill on bootstrap leader flag, not chain height

Using height==0 as the gate broke G1 restarts — after syncing to height N,
G1 would skip its own backfill and leave wallets with in-memory-only SOV
balances (balance=0 in BlockExecutor), causing TokenTransfer to fail with
"Insufficient token balance: have 0".

The correct gate is is_bootstrap_leader: only the node whose DID is first in
bootstrap_validators creates SOV backfill TokenMint transactions. Non-leader
validators get their balances by syncing the leader's mined blocks.

Pass is_bootstrap_leader through IdentityComponent constructors and mod.rs.

* feat(oracle): implement oracle committee bootstrap with persistent signing keys

- Add POST /api/v1/oracle/committee/propose endpoint that bootstraps the
  oracle committee directly when it is currently empty (gated by committee
  being uninitialized, subsequent changes require DAO governance)
- Add `oracle_signing_pubkeys: HashMap<[u8;32], Vec<u8>>` to OracleState
  to store actual dilithium public keys for attestation signature verification
  (genesis validator consensus_key != per-node dilithium_pk)
- Add ORACLE_STATE key and get/save_oracle_state methods to BlockchainStore
  trait with default no-op implementations for backward compatibility
- Implement direct (non-transactional) oracle_state persistence in SledStore
  using meta tree, allowing bootstrap outside block processing context
- In bootstrap_oracle_committee(), persist oracle_state to SledStore after
  setting committee so state survives node restarts
- In load_from_store(), restore oracle_state from SledStore at startup
- Update CLI oracle committee-update to call the new endpoint directly
  with --pubkeys flag for passing signing public keys
- Update oracle attestation consumer and producer to resolve signing keys
  from oracle_signing_pubkeys first, falling back to validator_registry

* fix(wallet): sync SOV balances from token_balances Sled tree into in-memory on startup

The BlockExecutor treats WalletRegistration as a legacy no-op and updates
token_balances tree directly for TokenMint/TokenTransfer. However,
put_token_contract (which updates the serialized contract blob) is called from
process_wallet_transactions post-executor-commit with no active Sled transaction,
causing it to silently fail. On restart, load_from_store loads the stale blob,
leaving token_contracts.balances empty for executor-path wallets. The wallet
list handler reads from token_contracts.balances and returns 0 for all wallets.

Fix: after repair_backfill_inflation corrects the token_balances Sled tree,
iterate wallet_registry and read each wallet's balance from the authoritative
token_balances tree, populating token_contracts.balances in-memory. This
ensures the wallet handler sees correct balances across restarts without
touching oracle state, consensus, or block processing paths.

Also suppress dead_code warning for activate_at_epoch field.

* fix: add missing creator_did argument to BondingCurveToken::deploy() call sites

Add String::new() as the creator_did parameter (#8) to all test
call sites in token.rs, registry.rs, and integration tests.
